title: Steak and ale pie
subtitle: Chuck steak braised in stout, suet-pastry lid.
excerpt: Cubes of chuck steak browned hard and slow-braised in stout with onions, mushrooms, and thyme until the gravy is dark and the meat falls. Spooned into a pie dish, topped with a suet pastry lid, and baked until the crust is golden. Serves four to six.
sourceNotes: Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861, Project Gutenberg #10136), 'Beef Steak Pie', as the suet-crust prototype. The use of stout in place of broth is a twentieth-century pub refinement; Beeton uses water and a glass of ale. USDA / NCHFP guidance on minimum braising temperatures (above 80°C for collagen breakdown).

recipe.freezeNotes: The filling alone freezes for three months. Cool, portion, freeze flat. Top with fresh pastry on the day. A whole baked pie reheats from the fridge at 170°C for 25 minutes.
recipe.batchNotes: Double the filling; the long braise scales well. The pie dish caps your portion size, not the cook.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Make the filling up to two days ahead and chill; the flavour deepens. Top and bake on the day.
recipe.temperatureNote: fan 180°C for the pie bake; braise on the lowest hob setting

recipeTools.notes: Heavy-based with a tight lid; the long braise depends on even, low heat.
Around 1.5 L capacity, deep enough for filling and a lid.

glossaryTerms.definition: A simple pastry made with self-raising flour and shredded suet (beef or vegetable). Lighter and steam-puffed compared to shortcrust; the traditional lid for British steak pies and steamed puddings.

content.text: Cubed chuck steak browned hard, then slow-braised for two and a half hours with onions, chestnut mushrooms, thyme, bay, and a generous pour of stout. The gravy reduces to a dark glossy sauce; the meat reaches the falling-tender state where a spoon parts it. Cooled, spooned into a deep pie dish, topped with a suet-pastry lid brushed with egg, baked until the crust is gold and the gravy bubbles through the steam slits.

Chuck is the right cut: well-marbled, full of connective tissue that breaks down to gelatine over a slow braise, making the gravy thick and silky as it cools. Cheaper cuts like shin work too with another hour added. Don't use sirloin or rump; they tighten and dry over the long cook.

items.prepNote: trimmed and cut into 3 cm cubes

content.text: Toss {{beef-chuck}} of cubed beef with {{plain-flour}} of flour, salt, and pepper. Heat {{rapeseed-oil}} of oil in a heavy Dutch oven over a hot hob. Brown the meat in three batches; don't crowd the pan or it steams. Each batch wants two minutes a side. Lift to a plate. The dark fond left in the pan is half the flavour of the gravy.

Drop the heat. Cook {{onion}} of sliced onions for 10 minutes until soft and golden. Add {{mushrooms-chestnut}} of mushrooms; cook 5 minutes, stirring, until they release their water and start to brown. Stir in {{garlic}} of crushed garlic; cook one more minute.

Stir in {{tomato-puree}} of tomato purée. Cook for a minute. Pour in {{stout}} of stout and scrape the fond off the base with a wooden spoon. Reduce by a third over high heat, 4 minutes. Return the beef and any resting juices. Pour in {{stock-beef}} of hot stock and {{worcestershire-sauce}} of Worcestershire. Tuck in the {{thyme}} of thyme and the {{bay-leaves}} of bay.

Bring to a bare simmer. Cover and cook on the lowest hob setting for 2 hours, or in a 150°C oven for the same time. Check now and then; if the gravy needs thickening at the end, simmer uncovered for 10 minutes. The meat should yield to a spoon. Cool the filling for at least 30 minutes — pouring hot filling into a pastry-lined dish melts the suet pastry into the sauce.

Combine {{self-raising-flour}} of self-raising flour, {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt, and {{lard}} of shredded suet or grated cold lard in a bowl. Stir in {{water}} of cold water with a knife to a soft dough. Bring together with your hands; don't overwork. Roll out on a floured board to 1 cm thick, big enough to cover your pie dish with overhang.
puffs in the oven and tears handsomely with a spoon.

Heat the oven to 200°C (180°C fan). Spoon the cooled filling into a 1.5 L pie dish. Brush the rim with beaten egg. Drape the pastry over the top. Trim the overhang to a finger's width and crimp with a fork. Cut a 5 cm slit in the middle to let steam escape. Brush all over with {{eggs}} beaten egg. Bake 25 minutes until the pastry is deep gold and the filling bubbles through the slit.

Rest the pie for 15 minutes after it comes out of the oven. The filling thickens; the pastry stays crisp. Serve with mashed potato and buttered greens; the gravy is rich enough that nothing else is needed.

items.cause: Wrong cut, or simmer too gentle.
items.fix: Use chuck or shin. Ensure a steady simmer; chuck wants 80°C+ to break down. Add another hour if needed.

items.cause: Lid trapped too much steam, or stock added too cold.
items.fix: Reduce uncovered for 10 minutes at the end. Always add hot stock to a hot pan.
Cool the filling for at least 30 minutes. Some cooks chill overnight.

items.cause: Pie too cold from the fridge when it went in.
items.fix: Bring to room temperature before baking, or bake at 180°C for 35 minutes from cold.
Reduce by a third, not half; the bitterness concentrates if you push it further.

content.text: Steak and ale is the gastropub icon of British pie cookery. Eaten on dark Friday evenings with a pint of the same stout that went into the gravy, it speaks of slow heat and patient cooking. The Victorian household manuals called it 'beef-steak pie' and used water; the stout came later, when British brewing met British baking and the two refused to part.
